plainMeaning: After teaching the deepest truths to steadfast people, the two Lords Ajita and Śānti passed through spiritual discipline to the flawless, liberated state — and I worship them, long and lovingly.
recitation: bodhayitvātha tattvāni dhīraṃ janaṃ, yogato yau gatau dhāma nīrañjanaṃ; tam aham ajitaṃ ca śāntiṃ ca cañcad-dyutiṃ, suciram arcāmi pañceṣu vijayodyatam.

wordByWord.gloss: having awakened, having taught, having made (others) understand
wordByWord.etymology: gerund (ktvā) of the causative bodhaya- from √budh 'to awaken, know'
wordByWord.notes: printed sandhi-joined with the following particle as બોધયિત્વાથ (bodhayitvā + atha)

wordByWord.etymology: Vedic/Skt connective atha; marks the temporal turn after the preaching scene
wordByWord.notes: the hinge from the teaching of the prior verses to the liberation told here

wordByWord.etymology: neuter acc. pl. of tattva, 'that-ness, essential principle'; from tat + -tva
wordByWord.notes: object of bodhayitvā — the Jain tattvas taught to seekers

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg. of dhīra, from √dhī 'to think, be steady'
wordByWord.notes: qualifies janaṃ; only the steadfast receive the tattvas

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg. (collective) of jana, from √jan 'to be born'
wordByWord.notes: the recipients awakened to the truths

wordByWord.etymology: ablative-adverbial yoga + -taḥ; here the culminating meditative discipline (dhyāna)
wordByWord.notes: printed યોગતો by visarga-sandhi before yau

wordByWord.etymology: nom. dual of the relative yad; the dual marks the Ajita–Śānti pair
wordByWord.notes: antecedent of the dual gatau and of tam below

wordByWord.gloss: the two went, the two reached
wordByWord.etymology: nom. dual of the past participle gata, from √gam 'to go'
wordByWord.notes: their passage to liberation — the nirvāṇa-kalyāṇaka

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg. of dhāman, from √dhā 'to set, hold'
wordByWord.notes: the goal reached; specified by nīrañjanaṃ

wordByWord.etymology: nis + añjana ('stain, collyrium') → nir-añjana, 'free of all stain'; from √añj 'to smear'
wordByWord.notes: names mokṣa as the state void of karmic añjana

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg. masc. of tad
wordByWord.notes: singular for the dual pair — the yamala device of worshipping the two as one; printed sandhi-joined as તમહમ-

wordByWord.etymology: nom. sg. first-person asmad
wordByWord.notes: the worshipper names himself — the personal vow of adoration

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg.; a + jita ('conquered'), from √ji 'to conquer'
wordByWord.notes: proper name, here resonating with vijayodyatam below

wordByWord.etymology: enclitic conjunction ca
wordByWord.notes: links Ajita and Śānti

wordByWord.etymology: acc. sg. of śānti 'peace', from √śam 'to be calm'
wordByWord.notes: the second Jina of the worshipped pair

wordByWord.etymology: enclitic conjunction ca
wordByWord.notes: the second ca completes the 'both A and B' pairing

wordByWord.etymology: bahuvrīhi: cañcat (pres. part. of intensive √cañc 'to flicker') + dyuti ('lustre', from √dyut 'to shine'); acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: epithet of the worshipped pair; a yamaka-chime with udyatam

wordByWord.gloss: for a very long time, long and lastingly
wordByWord.etymology: su- ('well, very') + cira ('long time'), adverbial acc.
wordByWord.notes: printed સુચિર before the sandhi cluster મર્ચામિ; extends the vow of worship

wordByWord.etymology: 1st sg. pres. of √arc 'to honour, praise'
wordByWord.notes: the main verb; printed with the carried-over m of suciram as -મર્ચામિ

wordByWord.gloss: over the five [arrows of Kāma / the five senses]
wordByWord.etymology: pañca ('five') + iṣu ('arrow') → pañceṣu; the five floral arrows of Kāma, the god of desire
wordByWord.notes: read as locative with vijaya ('victory over the five'); alternatively the bahuvrīhi pañca-iṣu = Kāmadeva himself, conquered

wordByWord.gloss: intent on victory, bent on conquest
wordByWord.etymology: vijaya ('victory', vi + √ji) + udyata ('raised up, intent', ud + √yam); acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: with pañceṣu: 'intent on conquering the five arrows of desire' — glossing the very name Ajita

literalTranslation: Having then awakened the steadfast folk to the truths, the two who by yoga went to the stainless abode — him, both Ajita and Śānti, of quivering radiance, intent on victory over the five [arrows of Kāma] — long do I worship.
idiomaticTranslation: Then, having awakened steadfast souls to the essential truths, the two Lords who through yoga passed to the flawless, liberated abode — Ajita and Śānti, ablaze with flashing splendour and ever bent on conquering the five arrows of desire — these I adore, and shall adore, for ages without end.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse carries the joint life-story to its close and turns it back upon the reciter. The opening 'atha' (then) is a quiet hinge: having just preached in the assembly, the two Jinas now complete the last of life's events. 'Bodhayitvā… dhīraṃ janaṃ' makes a pointed observation — the truths land only on the steadfast (dhīra); awakening presupposes a prepared mind. 'Yogato yau gatau dhāma nīrañjanaṃ' names liberation itself: it is by yoga — the culminating meditative absorption, not bodily striving — that they reach the 'stainless abode,' nirvāṇa described as nir-añjana, utterly free of karmic stain. The single 'tam' governing two Jinas is deliberate: the pair is adored as one. The closing epithet 'pañceṣu vijayodyatam' is the verse's sharpest stroke — these Lords are 'intent on victory over the five arrows' of Kāma, the god of desire; the line thereby unfolds the very name Ajita ('Unconquered'): they are unconquered precisely because they conquered desire. Against that, 'aham… suciram arcāmi' is the devotee's answering vow — 'I worship, and long shall worship' — the present tense stretched by 'suciram' into a lifelong act. The poet's sound-craft chimes throughout (janaṃ / nīrañjanaṃ; cañcad-dyutiṃ / vijayodyatam), so the line is felt as music while it states doctrine. The verse thus completes the arc — teaching, liberation, adoration — and sets up the protective promise that immediately follows.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Two Jain points are concentrated here. First, mokṣa as nir-añjana: liberation is the soul stripped of all karmic 'stain' (añjana), the siddha-state reached when karma is exhausted — the standard account of the soul's final destination. Second, the means: 'yogataḥ' locates the cause of release in dhyāna-yoga, the meditative absorption that severs the last karmas, not in outward ritual. The epithet 'vijayodyata over the five arrows' frames conquest of kāma and the senses as the precondition of Jinahood — sense-victory (jitendriyatva) is what 'Jina' and 'Ajita' literally encode. The pairing with 'tattva-bodha' shows the Tīrthaṅkara's twofold work: to teach the realities (jīva, ajīva, and the rest) and then to demonstrate their fruit by attaining the stainless state himself.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter does not merely describe the Jinas but names himself ('aham') and binds himself by a continuing vow ('suciram arcāmi') — worship as a sustained, lifelong discipline rather than a single utterance. The verse holds up a practicable order: steadiness (dhīra) makes one fit to receive truth; inner conquest of the five sensual 'arrows,' not ceremony alone, opens the stainless state. A practitioner reads it as a map — cultivate steadfastness, study the tattvas, master desire — and as an invitation to make adoration habitual.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha Sūtra 10.2–4 — liberation (mokṣa) as the total destruction of karma and the soul's ascent
Uttarādhyayana-sūtra, on conquest of the senses (jitendriyatva) as the mark of the victor
Companion Ajita-Śānti hymns of the same Smaraṇa set (Prakrit, 2nd; Laghu, 8th) that likewise yoke the two Jinas

sources.title: Nirañjana — 28 definitions (Sanskrit/Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of nir-añjana ('stainless') and its Jain use for the liberated/unstained state (dhāma nīrañjanaṃ)

sources.title: Tattva — definitions (Sanskrit/Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of tattva as 'fundamental reality/principle' for tattvāni bodhayitvā

sources.title: Pañceṣu / Pañcan-iṣu — 'five-arrowed' (Kāma)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming pañca-iṣu = the five floral arrows of Kāma, behind pañceṣu vijayodyatam

notes: Reading choices for THIS verse: (1) 'pañceṣu' is taken as a locative governed by vijaya ('victory over the five [arrows of Kāma]'), the desire-conquest theme that glosses the name Ajita; an equally defensible reading takes it as the bahuvrīhi pañca-iṣu = Kāmadeva as the object conquered. (2) The singular 'tam' for the dual pair (antecedent yau/gatau) is the deliberate yamala device of worshipping the two Jinas as one, not a grammatical slip. (3) Book orthography is preserved verbatim, including anusvāra spellings and the printed sandhi that splits words across the visual word-break (બોધયિત્વાથ = bodhayitvā+atha; તમહમજિતં = tam+aham+ajitaṃ; સુચિર મર્ચામિ = suciram+arcāmi). (4) Per the tradition knowledge base the Bṛhad Ajitaśānti's authorship is unattributed, and open-web attributions/translations of this hymn proved unreliable; the glosses therefore rest on standard Sanskrit lexicon and grammar rather than on any single published translation of this verse. (5) The verse shows pervasive yamaka/anuprāsa (janaṃ/nīrañjanaṃ; dyutiṃ/udyatam); the specific chandas name is not asserted for want of verification.
